did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a high-performing Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) named CNNTB (Convolutional Neural Network for Calculating Tumor Burden) to calculate tumor burden in H&E-stained cutaneous melanoma. The CNN was trained on a large annotated dataset generated via computer-assisted annotation of digital H&E/SOX10 dual stains. The study explicitly uses deep learning (CNN) as the core method to obtain quantitative results regarding tumor burden, comparing its performance against pathologists and stereological counts.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNNTB) for calculating tumor burden in cutaneous melanoma. It details specific methods including digital H&E/SOX10 dual staining, computer-assisted annotation of 1,221,367 nuclei, and performance evaluation against pathologists and stereological counts on a dataset of 77 melanoma cases. The structure includes standard research sections (Abstract, Introduction, Materials and Methods, Results, Discussion) and reports new quantitative findings and statistical comparisons.

title: Computer-Assisted Annotation of Digital H&E/SOX10 Dual Stains Generates High-Performing Convolutional Neural Network for Calculating Tumor Burden in H&E-Stained Cutaneous Melanoma
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
brief_summary: This study developed a high-performing Convolutional Neural Network (CNNTB) to calculate tumor burden in H&E-stained cutaneous melanoma using computer-assisted annotation of digital H&E/SOX10 dual stains. The training dataset comprised 1,221,367 annotated nuclei derived from 77 melanoma cases, including primary tumors and metastases, where SOX10 annotations were transferred to corresponding H&E images. The CNNTB achieved a weighted-average F1-score of 88.8% and demonstrated high precision for tumor and normal cells in primary melanomas. When compared to stereological counts, the mean difference in tumor burden was 6% for CNNTB versus 16% for pathologists' routine assessments. The results indicate that this IHC-supervised annotation technique generates a high-quality training set that enables superior automated tumor burden calculation compared to routine pathologist evaluations for primary melanomas and subcutaneous metastases.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary melanomas were the main focus for training and validating the CNNTB model. 30 excised primary melanomas were included in the specimen collection. The CNN achieved 100% precision for tumor cells in primary melanomas. Subcutaneous metastases were grouped with primary melanomas for high-performing annotation due to similar SOX10 positivity.

Subcutaneous metastases were included in the development and test of CNNTB because they exhibited high SOX10 positivity similar to primary melanomas. They were grouped with primary melanomas for the high-performing annotation analysis.

Lymph-node metastases were included in the dataset (n=15 total: 5 regional, 5 distant). However, precision for normal cells was markedly reduced in these lesions compared to primary melanomas due to low or missing tumor-cell SOX10 positivity (p < 0.001). They were not included in the final high-performing CNNTB development/test set.

paper_terms: distant metastases to either lymph nodes, subcutis, lung, liver, or brain
lesion_types_studied.details: Distant organ metastases (lung, liver, brain) were included in the dataset. Like lymph-node metastases, they showed low or missing tumor-cell SOX10 positivity, leading to reduced precision for normal cells. They were not included in the final high-performing CNNTB development/test set.
Two locoregional dermal metastases were included in the training set for the intermediate CNNAnn models. They were also part of the independent test set for CNNTB.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise classification of nuclei into tumor-cell nuclei, normal-cell nuclei, and background classes to calculate tumor burden.
all_task_types.details: The paper describes the development of CNNTB (Convolutional Neural Network for Calculating Tumor Burden) which performs segmentation of nuclei. Table 6 explicitly reports 'Segmentation Performance' metrics (Sensitivity/recall, Specificity, Accuracy, Precision, F1 score) for three classes: Tumor Nuclei, Normal Nuclei, and Background. The model predicts discrete class labels for each nucleus/pixel region rather than a continuous numeric value directly, although the ultimate goal is to calculate a continuous tumor burden percentage from these segmented counts.

datasets_used.name: Primary Melanoma and Subcutaneous Metastasis Cohort (CNNTB Training Set)
datasets_used.description: A cohort of primary cutaneous melanomas and subcutaneous melanoma metastases used to generate the high-performing CNNTB model. These lesions were digitized, re-stained with SOX10, and used for computer-assisted annotation to create a large training set of nuclei. The study notes that these lesion types exhibited high SOX10 positivity, making them suitable for this annotation method.

use.training: Used to train the CNNTB model. The dataset consisted of 13 primary melanomas and 6 subcutaneous metastases. Annotations were generated via SOX10 dual stains transferred to H&E images, resulting in 799,992 tumor cell nuclei, 421,375 normal cell nuclei (plus 112,291 dilated for cluster adjustment), 37,265 remainder tissue annotations, and 7,937 background annotations.
use.validation: Visual inspection of performance was examined on 11 independent primary melanomas and 5 independent melanoma metastases (3 subcutaneous, 2 distant lymph-node) before final testing.
use.other: The mean red-chromaticity level of annotated tumor cells in this group (n=15 combined primary and subcutaneous) was 0.37, significantly higher than in lymph-node/organ metastases.

datasets_used.name: CNNTB Independent Test Set
datasets_used.description: An independent set of primary melanomas and metastases used to evaluate the final CNNTB model's segmentation performance and tumor burden calculation accuracy against stereological counts.

use.testing: Used to test the CNNTB model. The set included 6 primary melanomas, 2 locoregional dermal metastases, and 3 subcutaneous metastases. The model achieved a weighted-average F1-score of 88.8% and mean tumor burden difference of 6% compared to stereological counts.

datasets_used.name: CNNAnn Training Set (Intermediate Models)
datasets_used.description: A dataset used to train the intermediate convolutional neural networks (CNNAnn-IHC and CNNAnn-H&E/IHC) for annotation purposes. These models were used to annotate the training set for CNNTB.

use.training: Used to train CNNAnn-IHC and CNNAnn-H&E/IHC. The set included 8 primary melanomas, 2 locoregional dermal metastases, 10 resections of melanoma metastases, and 10 core needle biopsies of melanoma metastases (7 regional lymph-node, 1 distant lymph-node, 4 subcutaneous, 2 lung, 4 liver, 2 brain). Approximately 1,100 subimages/training pairs were included, with 9,174 tumor cell nuclei, 10,698 normal cell nuclei, 7,711 remainder tissue annotations, and 304 background annotations.
use.other: The best-performing algorithm from this training (CNNAnn-H&E/IHC) was subsequently used to annotate the larger CNNTB training set.

datasets_used.name: CNNAnn Independent Test Set
datasets_used.description: An independent set of lesions used to evaluate the annotation capabilities of the CNNAnn models in metastatic lesions.

use.testing: Used to test the annotation capabilities of CNNAnn models. The set included 11 primary melanomas, 5 regional lymph-node metastases, 5 distant lymph-node metastases, 5 subcutaneous metastases, and 5 distant organ metastases (4 lung, 1 brain).

datasets_used.name: Thresholding Comparison Cohort
datasets_used.description: A subset of primary melanomas used to compare the utility of thresholding using either the red (Fast-Red) or brown (DAB) chromogen for IHC-supervised annotation.

use.testing: Used to compare THRbrown and THRred annotation performance in primary melanomas. THRred showed higher precision (99.5%) for tumor cells compared to THRbrown (89.9%).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Calculation of 95% confidence intervals (CI) for all proportions (precision, sensitivity, etc.).
To compare precision of annotation techniques (e.g., THRbrown vs. THRred) and to compare precision between primary melanomas and metastatic melanomas.
To investigate the difference between the mean red chromaticity level of primary melanomas and melanoma metastases.
To compare stereological counts with the tumor burden calculated by CNNTB or routine pathologist eyeballing.
To compare stereological counts with the tumor burden calculated by CNNTB or routine pathologist eyeballing, assessing agreement and bias.
Calculation of sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision, and weighted-average F1-score for CNNTB based on pixel classification.

hypothesis_testing.test: Two-sample tests of proportions, Unpaired t-tests, Paired t-tests
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in precision between annotation techniques (e.g., THRbrown vs. THRred); there is no difference in mean red chromaticity level between primary melanomas and melanoma metastases; there is no difference between stereological counts and tumor burden calculated by CNNTB or pathologists.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in precision between annotation techniques; there is a difference in mean red chromaticity level between primary melanomas and melanoma metastases; there is a difference between stereological counts and tumor burden calculated by CNNTB or pathologists.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Two-sided p-values less than 0.050 were considered statistically significant. Significant differences were found in precision between THRbrown and THRred (p < 0.001), in red chromaticity levels between primary/subcutaneous metastases and organ/lymph-node metastases (p = 0.002 and p < 0.001), and in precision for normal cells between primary melanomas and metastases (p < 0.001). The difference in tumor burden between CNNTB and stereological counts was not statistically significant (p = 0.10), whereas the difference for pathologists was significant (p = 0.02).

Primary Melanomas (n=22 for thresholding.notes: The difference in precision between THRbrown and THRred was -9.6% (95%CI, -11.2% to -8.0%; p < 0.001). The difference in precision between CNNAnn-H&E/IHC and THRred was not statistically significant, but false-positive annotations were considerably lower for CNNAnn-H&E/IHC.

Metastases (CNNAnn-H&E/IHC Test Set, n=2.notes: The difference in precision for primary melanoma versus metastatic melanoma was extremely similar for tumor nuclei (p = 1), but markedly reduced for normal-cell nuclei with a mean difference of 8.1% (95%CI, 6.3% to 9.9%; p < 0.001). Only subcutaneous metastases exhibited very few false-positive annotations along with an acceptable annotation rate for normal cells.

CNNTB Training Set (Primary Melanomas an.notes: Annotations were generated via SOX10 dual stains transferred to H&E images. The mean red-chromaticity level of annotated tumor cells in this group (n=15 combined primary and subcutaneous) was 0.37 (95%CI, 0.35 to 0.39).

CNNTB Independent Test Set (Primary Mela.metric: Segmentation Performance and Tumor Burden Accuracy
CNNTB Independent Test Set (Primary Mela.notes: For primary melanomas, precision of annotation was 100% (95%CI, 99% to 100%) for tumor cells and 99% (95%CI, 98% to 100%) for normal cells. The mean difference in tumor burden was 6% for CNNTB versus 16% for pathologists. The difference between CNNTB and stereological counts was not statistically significant (p=0.10), whereas the difference for pathologists was significant (p=0.02).

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (trained from scratch on annotated nuclei data)

variants.name: CNNAnn-H&E/IHC
variants.input_channels: 6 (RGB from both H&E and IHC stains)

variants.name: CNNTB
variants.input_channels: Not explicitly specified, but trained on data generated by CNNAnn-H&E/IHC
variants.description: Convolutional Neural Network for Calculating Tumor Burden

architecture_details.postprocessing: Mean filters, thresholding, morphological operations, watershed algorithms, and polynomial blob filters

training_iterations.CNNTB: 398,000 iterations selected (16 epochs) out of 613 total iterations

training_details.training_data_source: Computer-assisted annotation of digital H&E/SOX10 dual stains

training_data_counts.CNNAnn_Training: 9,174 tumor cell nuclei, 10,698 normal cell nuclei, 7,711 remainder tissue, 304 background (approx. 1,100 subimages)
training_data_counts.CNNTB_Training: 799,992 tumor cell nuclei, 421,375 normal cell nuclei (plus 112,291 dilated for clusters), 37,265 remainder tissue, 7,937 background (approx. 25,000 subimages)

classification_or_output_head.tumor_burden_calculation: Number-based percentage level (tumor-cell nuclei / all nuclei within outlined tumor area)

preprocessing_techniques: Image alignment of H&E and SOX10 dual stains to enable direct transfer of annotations
Color deconvolution to enhance red or brown staining colors
Red and blue color chromaticity and contrast preprocessing for thresholding applications
Application of mean filters to neural network feature maps
Thresholding of feature maps based on chromaticity and contrast
Watershed algorithms to separate cellular clusters into individual cells
Polynomial blob filters for postprocessing segmentation results

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Utilized during training of U-Net models (CNNAnn-IHC, CNNAnn-H&E/IHC, and CNNTB) in Visiopharm A/S Author AI. Specific parameters or techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, scaling) were not explicitly detailed in the text, but the paper notes that 'data augmentation was utilized' and refers to Supplementary Table S1 for specifics.

Additional manual annotations were included in the labeled training data to add variation. For CNNTB, nuclei of normal cells were dilated to form cellular clusters and included again to further adjust the model, resulting in an additional 112,291 normal-cell annotations.

conclusions: Computer-assisted annotation using digital H&E/SOX10 dual stains successfully generated a large, high-quality training set of 1,221,367 annotated nuclei for primary melanomas and subcutaneous metastases within a reasonable timeframe.

The developed Convolutional Neural Network for Calculating Tumor Burden (CNNTB) achieved a weighted-average F1-score of 88.8% and high precision (100% for tumor cells, 99% for normal cells) in primary melanomas.

CNNTB demonstrated superior performance compared to routine pathologist assessments, with a mean difference in tumor burden of 6% versus stereological counts for CNNTB compared to 16% for pathologists (p = 0.02 for pathologists vs. p = 0.10 for CNNTB).

The IHC-supervised annotation technique is limited by low or missing tumor-cell SOX10 positivity in lymph-node and distant organ metastases, which markedly reduced the precision for normal cells (p < 0.001) and prevented the generation of a high-performing model for these lesion types.

Including H&E stain information alongside IHC stains in the CNN input (CNNAnn-H&E/IHC) significantly improved annotation performance by eliminating errors related to unspecific SOX10 reactions and skewed alignments compared to using IHC stains alone.

The study concludes that this IHC-supervised annotation method is feasible and effective for generating high-performing deep learning models for tumor burden calculation in cutaneous melanomas and subcutaneous metastases, but not for metastases with low SOX10 expression.
important_remarks: The method is limited to primary melanomas and subcutaneous metastases because lymph-node and distant organ metastases exhibit low or missing SOX10 positivity, which markedly reduces annotation precision for normal cells (p < 0.001).
The study population is restricted to BRAF-positive tumors in the final testing phase, limiting the generalizability of the results to BRAF-negative melanomas.

The training dataset was generated using a labor-intensive process involving re-staining and re-scanning of tissue sections with SOX10, and the removal of coverslips was noted as difficult and time-consuming.

The CNNTB model was trained on data from a single institution (Aarhus University Hospital), raising potential concerns regarding external validation and generalizability to other populations or staining protocols.

The mean difference in tumor burden between CNNTB and stereological counts was not statistically significant (p = 0.10), whereas the difference for pathologists was significant (p = 0.02), indicating superior accuracy of the AI model over routine pathologist assessment.
The study utilized a proprietary software platform (Visiopharm A/S Author AI) for model development and training, which may impact reproducibility and independent verification.
Data availability is restricted to request from the corresponding author, limiting immediate independent validation of the training and testing datasets.
